clues: Evidence of this has been found in Egyptian mummies through physical signs of the disease as well as DNA in organs. It remains one of the top ten causes of death worldwide today
Also known as consumption historically due to the extreme weight loss it caused, concern about this disease played a role in the movement to prohibit public spitting
Edgar Allan Poe's wife was affected by this disease and began coughing up blood while singing, leading to him writing 'The Masque of the Red Death'
Developed in 1816 in Paris by Laennec, the market for this is now valued at over $630 million annually
While initially wooden tubes were used, rubber was introduced in the early 20th century, improving comfort and practical applications
Digital models of this device are now available that can amplify output up to 40 times more than traditional models

Before this was discovered, patients were treated with starvation diets, designed to prolong life by a few months. This medication was discovered and tested in 1922 and today, an estimated 8.3 million people in the United States require this medication

Interestingly, the predatory cone snail uses a modified version of this to paralyze nearby fish. These fish become lethargic and display erratic, uncoordinated movements allowing the snail to easily engulf and consume them. These symptoms are similar to what a human might display if given a large dose of this medication
While the original patent for this drug was only $1, this life-saving medication was on the news for being unaffordable until costs were capped at $35 per month for Medicare beneficiaries in 2022
In the top ten most prescribed medications in the United States, this drug is known for causing vivid dreams and erectile dysfunction
Although available in two forms, each has different uses and indications. Both act on beta-1 receptors.
Commonly used for heart failure and atrial fibrillation, this medication can also be used for migraine prophylaxis
Dionysius, tyrant of Heraclea (360-305BC) was affected by this disease and treated by physicians who would thrust fine needles through his ribs and belly whenever he fell asleep.
Up to 80-90% of cases may go undiagnosed, but this condition can increase risks of car accidents, hypertension, stroke, and heart failure

This condition was previously described as Pickwickian Syndrome based on a character 'Joe the Fat Boy' in a 1836 Charles Dickens novel, The Pickwick Papers. Joe was constantly sleepy, obese, and red faced. At that time, severe cases were originally treated with a tracheostomy
